title: Contributing to paretoscape
text: making contributions package R C++ code, make sure someone paretoscape team agrees change suggest needed. Fork package clone onto computer. haven’t done , recommend using usethis::create_from_github("josesalgr/paretoscape", fork = TRUE). Install development dependences devtools::install_dev_deps(), make sure package passes R CMD check running devtools::check(). R CMD check doesn’t pass cleanly, ’s good idea ask help continuing. Create Git branch pull request (PR). recommend using usethis::pr_init("brief-description--change"). Make changes, commit git, create PR running usethis::pr_push(), following prompts browser. title PR briefly describe change. body PR contain Fixes #issue-number. user-facing changes, add bullet top NEWS.md (.e. just first header). Follow style described https://style.tidyverse.org/news.html.

title: Contributing to paretoscape
text: opening pull request, please make sure : proposed change focused clearly described, package still passes devtools::check(), new functionality includes tests appropriate, user-facing changes include documentation updates, pull request body references related issue applicable. change affects package behaviour user perspective, please also add short bullet point NEWS.md recent development section.

title: Contributing to paretoscape
text: Please try keep contributions consistent current design paretoscape, especially modular structure: inputData() builds problem object, add_*() functions enrich problem, set_*() functions configure methods solver settings, solve() executes optimization workflow, get_*() plotting functions expose results. Changes preserve strengthen modular structure usually preferred monolithic shortcuts tightly coupled additions.

title: Multi-Objective Integrated Spatial Planning
text: multiscape exact optimisation framework multi-objective spatial planning R. designed planning problems spatial data, ecological socioeconomic features, constraints, multiple competing objectives must considered simultaneously within single decision-support workflow. package built around mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) formulations, allowing users represent spatial planning problems explicitly optimisation models solve exact methods. makes multiscape especially suitable applications transparent model structure, reproducibility, rigorous trade-analysis important. multiscape supports general spatial planning formulations action-based formulations decisions expressed management actions applied across planning units. , users can build planning problems tabular spatial inputs, define feasible actions effects, add targets constraints, register multiple objectives cost, benefit, profit, fragmentation, explore exact trade-offs using multi-objective methods weighted-sum, epsilon-constraint, AUGMECON.

text: multiscape brings together several key components spatial planning single optimisation framework: exact MILP-based modelling rigorous spatial decision support, multi-objective optimisation analyse trade-offs instead single optimum, spatially explicit formulations targets, constraints, spatial relations, flexible decision structures, including planning-unit action-based formulations, reproducible R workflow building solving optimisation problems.

title: Multi-Objective Integrated Spatial Planning
text: typical multiscape workflow five steps. Conceptual overview multiscape workflow, problem definition actions constraints, objectives, optimization, outputs. First, user creates Problem object planning units, features, baseline feature amounts. Second, user adds feasible actions , relevant, defines actions affect features interest. Third, user adds targets, constraints, spatial relations. Fourth, user registers one atomic objectives. Finally, user solves problem single-objective multi-objective mode. words, multiscape separates problem definition optimization. Problem object stores planning specification, solving stage later translates specification one exact optimization runs.

title: Multi-Objective Integrated Spatial Planning
text: multiscape currently supports several exact multi-objective workflows. weighted-sum method combines multiple registered objectives single scalar objective user-defined weights. simple useful preference-driven exploration trade-offs. epsilon-constraint method optimizes one objective directly transforming remaining objectives explicit performance constraints. especially useful one objective prioritised others controlled thresholds. AUGMECON method extends epsilon-constraint augmented formulation designed reduce weakly efficient solutions improve Pareto-front generation. Together, methods allow users move beyond single objective solution instead analyse sets efficient trade-solutions.

title: Add management actions to a planning problem — add_actions
code: # ------------------------------------------------------ # Minimal planning problem # ------------------------------------------------------ pu <- data.frame(   id = 1:4,   cost = c(2, 3, 1, 4) )  features <- data.frame(   id = 1:2,   name = c("sp1", "sp2") )  dist_features <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4),   feature = c(1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2),   amount = c(1, 2, 1, 3, 2, 1) )  p <- create_problem(   pu = pu,   features = features,   dist_features = dist_features )  actions <- data.frame(   id = c("conservation", "restoration"),   name = c("Conservation", "Restoration") )  # Example 1: all actions feasible in all planning units p1 <- add_actions(   x = p,   actions = actions,   cost = c(conservation = 5, restoration = 12) )  print(p1) #> A multiscape object (<Problem>) #> ├─data #> │├─planning units: <data.frame> (4 total) #> │├─costs: min: 1, max: 4 #> │└─features: 2 total ("sp1", "sp2") #> └─actions and effects #> │├─actions: 2 total ("Conservation", "Restoration") #> │├─feasible action pairs: 8 feasible rows #> │├─action costs: min: 5, max: 12 #> │├─effect data: none #> │└─profit data: none #> └─spatial #> │├─geometry: none #> │├─coordinates: none #> │└─relations: none #> └─targets and constraints #> │├─targets: none #> │├─area constraints: none #> │├─budget constraints: none #> │├─planning-unit locks: none #> │└─action locks: none #> └─model #> │├─status: not built yet (will build in solve()) #> │├─objectives: none #> │├─method: single-objective #> │├─solver: not set (auto) #> │└─checks: incomplete (no objective registered) #> # ℹ Use `x$data` to inspect stored tables and model snapshots. utils::head(p1$data$dist_actions) #>   pu       action cost status internal_pu internal_action #> 1  1 conservation    5      0           1               1 #> 5  1  restoration   12      0           1               2 #> 2  2 conservation    5      0           2               1 #> 6  2  restoration   12      0           2               2 #> 3  3 conservation    5      0           3               1 #> 7  3  restoration   12      0           3               2  # Example 2: specify feasible pairs explicitly include_df <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 2, 3, 4),   action = c("conservation", "conservation", "restoration", "restoration") )  p2 <- add_actions(   x = p,   actions = actions,   include_pairs = include_df,   cost = 10 )  p2$data$dist_actions #>   pu       action cost status internal_pu internal_action #> 1  1 conservation   10      0           1               1 #> 2  2 conservation   10      0           2               1 #> 3  3  restoration   10      0           3               2 #> 4  4  restoration   10      0           4               2  # Example 3: remove selected pairs after full expansion exclude_df <- data.frame(   pu = c(2, 4),   action = c("restoration", "conservation") )  p3 <- add_actions(   x = p,   actions = actions,   exclude_pairs = exclude_df,   cost = c(conservation = 3, restoration = 8) )  p3$data$dist_actions #>   pu       action cost status internal_pu internal_action #> 1  1 conservation    3      0           1               1 #> 5  1  restoration    8      0           1               2 #> 2  2 conservation    3      0           2               1 #> 3  3 conservation    3      0           3               1 #> 7  3  restoration    8      0           3               2 #> 8  4  restoration    8      0           4               2

1) Constant profit for every feasible (pu, action) p1 <- add_profit(p, profit = 10) p1$data$dist_profit #>   pu      action profit internal_pu internal_action #> 1  1     harvest     10           1               1 #> 5  1 restoration     10           1               2 #> 2  2     harvest     10           2               1 #> 6  2 restoration     10           2               2 #> 3  3     harvest     10           3               1 #> 7  3 restoration     10           3               2 #> 4  4     harvest     10           4               1 #> 8  4 restoration     10           4               2  # 2) Profit per action using a named vector pr <- c(harvest = 50, restoration = -5) p2 <- add_profit(p, profit = pr) p2$data$dist_profit #>   pu      action profit internal_pu internal_action #> 1  1     harvest     50           1               1 #> 5  1 restoration     -5           1               2 #> 2  2     harvest     50           2               1 #> 6  2 restoration     -5           2               2 #> 3  3     harvest     50           3               1 #> 7  3 restoration     -5           3               2 #> 4  4     harvest     50           4               1 #> 8  4 restoration     -5           4               2  # 3) Profit per action using a data frame pr_df <- data.frame(   action = c("harvest", "restoration"),   profit = c(40, 15) ) p3 <- add_profit(p, profit = pr_df) p3$data$dist_profit #>   pu      action profit internal_pu internal_action #> 1  1     harvest     40           1               1 #> 2  1 restoration     15           1               2 #> 3  2     harvest     40           2               1 #> 4  2 restoration     15           2               2 #> 5  3     harvest     40           3               1 #> 6  3 restoration     15           3               2 #> 7  4     harvest     40           4               1 #> 8  4 restoration     15           4               2  # 4) Profit per (pu, action) pair pr_pair <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 2, 3),   action = c("harvest", "harvest", "restoration"),   profit = c(100, 80, 30) ) p4 <- add_profit(p, profit = pr_pair) p4$data$dist_profit #>   pu      action profit internal_pu internal_action #> 1  1     harvest    100           1               1 #> 3  2     harvest     80           2               1 #> 6  3 restoration     30           3               2

title: Set the AUGMECON multi-objective method — set_method_augmecon
text: Use method one objective optimized directly, remaining objectives controlled epsilon levels, weakly efficient solutions reduced augmented formulation. General idea Suppose \(m \ge 2\) objective functions already registered problem: $$ f_1(x), f_2(x), \dots, f_m(x). $$ AUGMECON selects one primary objective, say \(f_p(x)\), treats remaining \(m - 1\) objectives secondary objectives. fixed combination epsilon levels, method solves sequence single-objective subproblems form: $$ \max \; f_p(x) + \rho \sum_{k \\mathcal{S}} \frac{s_k}{R_k} $$ subject $$ f_k(x) - s_k = \varepsilon_k, \qquad k \\mathcal{S}, $$ $$ s_k \ge 0, \qquad k \\mathcal{S}, $$ together original feasibility constraints planning problem. : \(f_p(x)\) primary objective, \(\mathcal{S}\) set secondary objectives, \(\varepsilon_k\) imposed level secondary objective \(k\), \(s_k\) non-negative slack variable, \(R_k\) payoff-table range used normalize objective \(k\), \(\rho > 0\) small augmentation coefficient. original AUGMECON formulation Mavrotas (2009), augmentation term ensures , among solutions primary objective value, solver prefers larger normalized slack, thereby avoiding weakly efficient points improving Pareto-front generation. Secondary-objective equalities slacks key difference standard epsilon-constraint AUGMECON secondary objectives written equalities slacks rather simple inequalities. maximization-type secondary objective, takes form: $$ f_k(x) - s_k = \varepsilon_k, \qquad s_k \ge 0. $$ implies: $$ f_k(x) \ge \varepsilon_k, $$ explicitly measuring excess imposed epsilon level \(s_k\). augmentation term rewards excess normalized form. implementation terms, exact sign convention objective depends whether internally treated minimization maximization objective, method always preserves AUGMECON principle: one objective optimized directly, others turned constrained objectives, non-negative slacks measure controlled deviation imposed epsilon levels, primary objective augmented small slack-based reward. Manual automatic epsilon grids AUGMECON requires grid epsilon levels secondary objective. grid supplied, must named list one numeric vector per secondary objective. vector defines exact epsilon levels explored objective. grid = NULL, grid generated automatically later solve. case, method first computes extreme points payoff-table ranges secondary objectives, generates n_points levels one. include_extremes = TRUE, automatic grid includes extreme values secondary objective. lexicographic = TRUE, extreme points computed using lexicographic anchoring, can improve payoff-table quality objectives tightly competing. tolerance used lexicographic anchoring controlled lexicographic_tol. Normalization augmentation augmentation term scaled using payoff-table ranges secondary objectives. \(R_k\) denotes range secondary objective \(k\), effective coefficient applied slack : $$ \frac{\rho}{R_k}, $$ \(\rho = \code{augmentation}\). normalization important different objectives may measured different numerical scales. Without normalization, slack belonging large-scale objective dominate augmentation term simply due units. implementation, user supplies augmentation base coefficient \(\rho\), normalized slack coefficients computed internally solve time using corresponding payoff-table ranges. code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # ------------------------------------------------------------ # Example 1: cost vs fragmentation # ------------------------------------------------------------ pu <- data.frame(id = 1:4, cost = c(1, 2, 2, 3)) features <- data.frame(id = 1, name = "sp1") dist_features <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 2, 3, 4),   feature = 1,   amount = c(1, 1, 1, 1) )  x <- create_problem(   pu = pu,   features = features,   dist_features = dist_features )  bnd <- data.frame(   id1 = c(1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 4),   id2 = c(1, 2, 3, 2, 3, 4, 4),   boundary = c(2, 2, 2, 1, 1, 1, 2) )  x <- add_spatial_boundary(x, boundary = bnd, name = "boundary")  x <- x |>   add_constraint_targets_relative(0.5) |>   add_objective_min_cost(alias = "cost") |>   add_objective_min_fragmentation(     alias = "frag",     relation_name = "boundary",     weight_multiplier = 0.01   )  x <- set_method_weighted_sum(   x,   aliases = c("cost", "frag"),   weights = c(1, 1),   normalize_weights = TRUE )  # sol <- solve(x)  # ------------------------------------------------------------ # Example 2: scan weights to explore trade-offs # ------------------------------------------------------------ weight_grid <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.25) xs <- vector("list", length(weight_grid))  for (i in seq_along(weight_grid)) {   w <- weight_grid[i]   xs[[i]] <- set_method_weighted_sum(     x,     aliases = c("cost", "frag"),     weights = c(1 - w, w),     normalize_weights = TRUE   )   # sols[[i]] <- solve(xs[[i]]) }  # ------------------------------------------------------------ # Example 3: request objective scaling # ------------------------------------------------------------ x <- set_method_weighted_sum(   x,   aliases = c("cost", "frag"),   weights = c(0.7, 0.3),   normalize_weights = FALSE,   objective_scaling = TRUE ) } # }

title: Solve a planning problem — solve
code: # ------------------------------------------------------------ # Minimal single-objective example # ------------------------------------------------------------ pu <- data.frame(   id = 1:4,   cost = c(1, 2, 3, 4) )  features <- data.frame(   id = 1:2,   name = c("sp1", "sp2") )  dist_features <- data.frame(   pu = c(1, 1, 2, 3, 4),   feature = c(1, 2, 2, 1, 2),   amount = c(5, 2, 3, 4, 1) )  actions <- data.frame(   id = c("conservation", "restoration") )  effects <- data.frame(   action = rep(c("conservation", "restoration"), each = 2),   feature = rep(features$id, times = 2),   multiplier = c(0.10, 0.10, 0.50, 0.50) )  x <- create_problem(   pu = pu,   features = features,   dist_features = dist_features ) |>   add_actions(     actions = actions,     cost = c(conservation = 1, restoration = 2)   ) |>   add_effects(     effects = effects,     effect_type = "after"   ) |>   add_constraint_targets_relative(0.05) |>   add_objective_min_cost(alias = "cost")  if (requireNamespace("rcbc", quietly = TRUE)) {   x <- set_solver_cbc(x, verbose = FALSE)   sol <- solve(x)   print(sol) } #> A multiscape solution (<Solution>) #> ├─result #> │├─status: .pa_solution_status_inline(st) #> │├─objective value: "3" #> │├─gap: 0% #> │└─runtime: 0.01 sec #> └─selection #> │├─planning units: 1 of 4 selected #> │├─actions: 1 of 8 selected #> │└─targets met: 2 of 2 #> └─objective values #> │└─cost: 3 #> └─solver #> │├─name: `cbc` #> │├─cores: 2 #> │└─time limit: 2147483647 #> # ℹ Use `x$summary` to inspect user-facing solution summaries.  # ------------------------------------------------------------ # Minimal multi-objective example # ------------------------------------------------------------ x_mo <- create_problem(   pu = pu,   features = features,   dist_features = dist_features ) |>   add_actions(     actions = actions,     cost = c(conservation = 1, restoration = 2)   ) |>   add_effects(     effects = effects,     effect_type = "after"   ) |>   add_constraint_targets_relative(0.05) |>   add_objective_min_cost(alias = "cost") |>   add_objective_max_benefit(alias = "benefit") |>   set_method_weighted_sum(     aliases = c("cost", "benefit"),     weights = c(0.5, 0.5),     normalize_weights = TRUE   )  if (requireNamespace("rcbc", quietly = TRUE)) {   x_mo <- set_solver_cbc(x_mo, verbose = FALSE)   solset <- solve(x_mo)   print(solset) } #> A multiscape solution set (<SolutionSet>) #> ├─method #> │├─name: `weighted` #> │└─objectives: 2 (cost, benefit) #> └─content #> │├─design rows: 1 #> │├─runs: 1 #> │└─solutions: 1 #> └─run summary #> │├─statuses: optimal: 1 #> │├─runtime: 0 #> │├─gap: 0 #> │├─design cols: none #> │└─value cols: value_cost, value_benefit #> # ℹ Use `x$solution$runs`, `x$solution$design`, and `x$solution$solutions[[i]]` #> to inspect details.

title: multiscape 1.0.4
text: First CRAN release multiscape. Provides modular workflow exact multi-objective spatial planning based mixed-integer programming (MIP). Introduces core Problem, Solution, SolutionSet classes. Adds support modular problem construction create_problem(), add_*(), set_*(), solve(). Supports atomic objective registration multi-objective solution methods, including weighted-sum, epsilon-constraint, AUGMECON. Includes support spatial relations boundary, rook, queen, k-nearest neighbours, distance-based relations. Supports commercial open-source solvers, including Gurobi, CPLEX, CBC, SYMPHONY. Adds user-facing extraction visualization tools planning units, actions, features, targets, spatial outputs, trade-offs. Includes substantial updates documentation, package structure, contribution guidelines.
